data.title: If Powell Is Out... The Ship Sinks
data.description:
Since the pandemic began, the U.S. economy has faced significant challenges that have disrupted the macroeconomic environment in just a few quarters. Interest rates have gone from near zero to one of the fastest increases in history due to rampant inflation, and then back down again. All this has been complicated by wars, significant fiscal deficits, and three technical bear markets (including a recession).

The stability of the dollar has been questioned on several occasions, but the Fed has done an excellent job of maintaining the attractiveness of the U.S. currency around the world. This is not a given after the record stimulus package to combat Covid-19.

Since Jerome Powell became Fed Chair (February 2, 2018) until the start of Trump's second term, the S&P 500 (SP500) has doubled and the Dollar Index (DXY) has not been this high in a long time. Powell, supported by the other members of the FOMC, has done an outstanding job and is one of the reasons why the stock market has performed so well in recent years.

In a world full of chaos, the Fed has managed to be a guiding light for the financial markets, and the only mistake was probably underestimating inflation in 2022. Overall, we cannot consider Powell's performance to be negative, yet rumors of his dismissal are growing louder.

The divergence between Powell and President Trump

Until the end of last year, it seemed that the worst was behind the Fed, but now the gray swan has arrived: the new U.S. president wants to impose tariffs that are the highest in 100 years. This is a major problem for currency stability, as tariffs tend to fuel inflation, the main enemy in previous years.

From normalizing rates by gradually reducing them, the Fed has been forced to suspend this process, at least until the effects of the tariffs are clearer. For Trump this is a serious mistake, as he sees double-digit tariffs as a weapon to reduce the U.S. trade deficit without fueling inflation. So, if they do not generate inflation, what is the point of keeping interest rates high? They are just a burden on the country's economic growth.

Furthermore, the fact that the ECB has cut interest rates for eight meetings in a row appears to further anger the president, as the rest of the world is easing while the Fed is not.

Furthermore, there is another fundamental reason why Trump is calling for a rapid cut in interest rates: the U.S. needs to refinance tens of trillions of dollars of debt over the next few years. It can no longer leverage the short end of the curve; it needs the long end to improve debt sustainability. But there is a problem: interest rates have broken through the 5% threshold.

By rolling over its debt at these rates, the U.S. government is basically set to pay billions and billions of dollars more in interest: money that could have been used in a more productive way. Trump is feeling the debt burden, which is why he is trying so hard to convince Powell to cut rates: his pressure has been mounting since April.

On the other hand, we have Powell, Chair of the Fed who has done a great job so far and does not want to ruin the last months of his term. The reason why he does not want to reduce rates is exclusively due to tariffs, as he himself mentioned:

In effect, we went on hold when we saw the size of the tariffs, and essentially all inflation forecasts for the United States went up materially as a consequence of the tariffs. We didn’t overreact. In fact, we didn’t react at all; we’re simply taking some time.

As long as the U.S. economy is in solid shape, the prudent thing to do is to wait and learn more and see what the effect might be. We haven’t seen effects much from tariffs, and we didn’t expect to by now. We have always said the timing, amount and persistence would be highly uncertain.

In other words, the fact that inflation has not already skyrocketed does not mean that the rates are not inflationary. These types of mechanisms take time to influence a country's economy, not a few months, so it is still too early to draw conclusions. Cutting rates today and raising them again in a few months (if tariff inflation does materialize) would send a negative signal to the markets: the Fed has no idea what is going on.

On the contrary, maintaining a cautious approach and then making a definitive decision is certainly a better way to convey a sense of control over the situation: the Fed expected prices to rise and will now act as planned. Powell can do all this because he is supported by a strong economy with very low unemployment.

Therefore, there is a clear divergence between Trump's vision and Powell's, and this is what worries me the most. Trump does not accept Powell's unwillingness to cut rates, and rumors of his dismissal are growing. At first, it seemed like a remote possibility, but today it is becoming increasingly concrete.

The New York Times reported that President Trump showed a group of Republicans a draft of Powell's firing letter, while Bloomberg said that a White House official said the dismissal would happen soon. Trump denied these reports but did not completely close the door on the matter:

I don't rule out anything, but I think it's highly unlikely unless he has to leave for fraud.

But what are his words referring to? What potential fraud could Powell have committed? The White House and Republican lawmakers are criticizing the excessive costs of renovating the Fed's headquarters in Washington. They are estimated to have reached $2.5 billion, a figure considered excessive and which is fueling some doubts. We will certainly know more about it in the coming weeks.

This could be Trump's lever to proceed with the dismissal; after all, he cannot do so simply because of differences of opinion. It is not enough that he is dissatisfied with Powell's performance to fire him; he needs a just cause, such as a fraud. My belief is that if Trump could, he would have already fired Powell, and the only reason he hasn't is that he hasn't found a "just cause" yet.

The rumors are growing louder despite denials, but what would Powell's dismissal mean for the markets?

The economic/monetary consequences of Powell's dismissal

If Powell is dismissed, his successor will be appointed by Trump and eventually approved by the Senate. Obviously, given that Trump wants a significant rate cut, Powell's successor will be someone who shares the President's view on this matter. But even before he sets foot in the Fed, I believe there will be three immediate effects on the financial markets:

  • The first is a sharp devaluation of the dollar.
  • The second is a rise in U.S. bond yields.
  • The third is a stock market crash.

These are just personal assumptions, but now I will explain why I am pessimistic following Powell's dismissal.

The first and second points are closely related. The independence of a central bank from the government is one of the crucial factors for a currency to be respected and appreciated around the world. Monetary policy must be carried out by people who are competent in the field and who are not in any way influenced by what they are told from outside. Every decision must be made for the long-term good of the currency, and sometimes this means swallowing bitter medicine in the short term. No one likes to see interest rates rise or remain high, but it is necessary to avert worse problems.

Firing the Fed chair at this stage of the market would send a negative signal about the Fed's independence and, consequently, about the stability of the dollar. Foreign investor confidence would plummet, and not only would dollars be sold, but U.S. bonds would also be sold. Then, if tariffs were to fuel inflation, I would expect a period of stagflation. Bond yields would skyrocket, and along with the devaluation of the dollar, this would create significant losses for foreign investors in U.S. debt.

Paradoxically, the decision to fire Powell to lower interest rates and refinance maturing US debt would prove to be a massive boomerang. I believe that the U.S. would then find itself in a worse situation overall. Now let's look at the impact on the stock market.

Firstly, the most obvious impact is that companies would pay higher interest rates: the companies with the highest levels of debt would be worst affected. Secondly, companies that import more than they export would face a challenging environment due to the weakness of the dollar. Thirdly, a country in stagflation means that consumers are struggling to spend, which would have a significant impact on GDP growth. If GDP falls, it means we are in recession, and the stock market cannot perform well in that context (economic decline + high bond yields).

At this point you might be thinking: all this fuss just because someone got fired? Well, yes, because replacing the Chair of such an important independent institution (who has done a good job so far) sends a strong signal of instability to the country and has direct consequences on the credibility of its currency abroad. The problem is that the successor will inevitably have a similar ideology to Trump (having been proposed by him), and the latter believes that the Fed should lower interest rates by at least 3%. A cut of this magnitude is almost impossible given that the FOMC is not composed solely of the Chair (there are other people involved in the decision-making process), but there would be a strong incentive to cut more than necessary. This factor, combined with tariff inflation, could be the basis for a perfect storm.

Clearly, my hope is that this will not happen and that Powell will remain in office until the end of his term, continuing to be unaffected by outside influences. However, if you think that the dynamics I have described represent too pessimistic a future, then you will find the next image interesting. At first glance, it may seem confusing, but I will explain it as simply as possible.

The blue line represents the differential between the ECB and Fed's rate expectations for June 2026. In simple terms, it shows the difference between the ECB and Fed interest rates in about a year's time. The red line is the EUR/USD exchange rate. As you can see, the red line and the blue line tend to have a positive correlation, although not always of the same magnitude: when one rises, the other rises too and vice versa. But take a look at the last few months: a huge gap has opened up.

As mentioned by Trump, the ECB has cut rates much more than the Fed, which is why the blue line is so low. When one central bank cuts rates more than another, the typical market movement is a devaluation of its currency. The reason is simple: if you lower rates and the other central bank does not, the yield on your bonds decreases, there is less capital inflows and therefore less demand for your currency. Following this academic reasoning and the correlation of recent years, the euro should have depreciated against the dollar since the ECB cut rates much more than the Fed, but the exact opposite happened. Why is there a historical divergence today? The reason lies in the components that influence the exchange rate.

Between two highly credible central banks such as the ECB and the Fed, expectations about future interest rates are the main factor affecting the exchange rate, but they are not the only one: components that were considered secondary in the past are now taking on greater importance. Until last year, no one questioned the Fed's independence, but today it is a recurring theme. Until last year, no one would have thought that trade relations with the rest of the world would change so drastically, yet they have. Until last year, few would have thought that fiscal deficits would worsen so much, yet they have.

Debt sustainability, trade relations with the world, and the independence of the Fed are issues that are undermining the credibility of the dollar, and investors are seeing the euro as a currency with less uncertainty, despite all its problems. So, the gap you see in the chart represents all the mistrust that the dollar is currently facing compared to the euro. The ECB can also bring rates to 0%, but the dollar will not appreciate until the United States operates in a more predictable manner.

What we are experiencing is a situation that can be resolved by a power such as the U.S., but in my opinion, Powell's dismissal would be a huge mistake that could further sink the dollar and the financial markets as a whole.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ASML: Of Course It's Little Down
data.description:
I first covered ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML) almost a year ago and rated it a Hold, mostly due to its valuation. With Q2 2025 earnings out, the stock was down about 8%, amid concerns about a lack of 2026 guidance for growth.

I can't say I was too surprised. I didn't think ASML was priced to be a Buy last year, and I think Q2 shows why that is still the case.

Summary of Previous Thesis

Titled Priced For Growth That We Can't Assume, my original thesis ceded ASML's leading role in the semiconductor industry. That role is in the creation of lithographic machines. Lithography entails transferring patterns onto silicon wafers using light. It's a sophisticated process, and major chip makers depend on ASML's product. This includes Intel Corporation (INTC), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), and Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF).

I did discuss the company's growing exposure to business in China, a more recent feature of their financial results, but I didn't see it as terribly risky in the long run, since there would be much global demand to satisfy if something troubled access to Chinese markets.

Where I took issue was the valuation relative to the cash flow. The market cap was $350B for a company that had never exceeded free cash flow of €10B. Even with the higher range of FCF and at more favorable exchange rates between the USD and EUR (seen below) this was a growth multiple of around 30 at the lowest, not exactly a discount.

I added that much of AMSL's historical gains depended on multiple expansion. Valuing ASML fairly around $463 per share, I concluded:

I don't think there are long-term risks of being wiped out, as a sophisticated product at scale has barriers to entry for potential competitors. That said, there are still forces that can affect demand that ASML does not control. On top of that, the business is currently priced for a bright future, but not for a dimmer rate of earnings growth.

Market Concerns about Q2 Outlook

While the stock is down about 14% since I covered it last year, most of that occurred in just the past day.

During the Q2 earnings call, CFO Roger Dassen articulated some concerns:

With regard to tariffs, the direct impact results from tariffs related to system sales to our customers in the United States, the import of materials for our U.S. manufacturing facilities, the import of parts and tools for our U.S. field operations and the export of parts from the U.S. into other countries to the extent tariffs were to apply to those parts...The indirect impact is more complex and very difficult to determine as it is related to the potential impact of tariffs on GDP and the resulting overall market demand.

In just a few sentences, the CFO spells out how the tariff impacts for this non-US business can be quite substantial. This caused ASML to pause any guidance for growth in 2026, hence the market's bad reaction.

This can explain, to some degree, the fluctuation of net sales by geography between Q1 and Q2 alone. As Q2 reflects initial impacts of tariffs, there is less in the US and more in the likes of Taiwan. As the tariff issue and ultimate impact on supply chains haven't been settled yet, it's not clear how disruptive this will prove to be for them.

What's more likely to affect them is how tariffs would impact their own ability to source materials, potentially squeezing gross margins, which had started to increase going into 2025. Given how much demand there is for chips to build out the infrastructure, I'd be less worried about pricing and volume, by comparison. An increase in cost of sales by, say, 10% won't delete gross profits, but it does have a meaningful impact on returns when the valuation is already high.

Valuation

So let's talk about that. In my last thesis, I gave the following valuation with a Discounted Cash Flow model (in EUR).

My FCF estimate was based on a five-year average. Before we consider whether we need to make any major changes here, let's look at the current financials and market price.

As the slide above shows, ASML's operating cash flows fluctuate wildly across quarters. As I discussed last time, this depends on when accounts receivable are actually monetized. TTM FCF therefore comes to about €9.2B. If one considers tighter gross margins from tariffs, that can easily shrink to €7B or €8B going forward.

Based on current exchange rates between USD and EUR, the $318B market cap on ASML gives a TTM Price/FCF multiple of 29.7, versus 39 if one goes with the €7B figure, and both of these exceed even an updated (for 2024) five-year average of €6.6B. These are not low prices, and thus they are sensitive to any news that is less than positive.

The reasons for a price contraction therefore become a bit clearer. The shares were already trading at a premium multiple. I could adjust my assumptions with the current data and get a fair value much closer to $550 per share, which is higher than the $463 I gave last year, but the bottom line is that ASML is priced for excellent execution, not for bumps in the road or its usual ebb and flow.

In other words, it's a fair value at best, but if some of the problems that were warned about in Q2 earnings materialize, there's no margin of safety.

Conclusion

I won't bet against a stock like this. I also won't hurry to buy it when such a capital-intensive business, even one in dominant position for its industry, is vulnerable to disruptive factors beyond its control, tariffs being just one of potentially many. The problem with many stocks like ASML is that the growth story of AI has gotten ahead of the risk factors. That was my basic thought process last year, and perhaps Q2's announcement confirms that outlook somewhat.

Until the share price declines more to reflect the risk factors, however, I maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: AppLovin: The Ad Tech Underdog Outperforming Competitors
data.description:
Introduction

AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) delivered another set of exceptional results in the first quarter of FY25, with sustained revenue growth and continued margin expansion. The company continues to benefit from its unparalleled AXON platform, which is unlocking better returns on advertising spend and facilitating efficient bidding processes for mobile game developers.

Following a divestiture of the firm's mobile gaming IP, AppLovin has transformed into a business exclusively focused on delivering high-margin software solutions. I believe that this shift will act as a positive inflection point, given that the market oftentimes rewards pure-play software companies with high valuation multiples.

The strong quarterly results, along with new innovations such as the upcoming self-service dashboard, promise to act as catalyzing forces that should push the stock higher. While competition from other players in the industry remains a risk, I believe that AppLovin offers solid upside potential since the intrinsic value of the business is greater than the current market capitalization. Therefore, I am rating the stock a BUY.

Business Description & Catalysts

AppLovin operates in the digital advertising industry with a focus on the mobile gaming industry; however, the company is rapidly expanding into new verticals. AppLovin's unique value proposition to customers is its AI-powered AXON platform, which helps advertisers automatically bid for ad placements and maximize their return on advertising spend (ROAS).

AppLovin also works behind the scenes to facilitate the bidding process, allowing mobile app developers to monetize their apps effectively while simultaneously helping advertisers reach their target audience.

The company operates through two primary arms, which are the Advertising and Apps segments. The Advertising division includes AppDiscovery, MAX, Adjust, and Wurl, which are each designed to either help advertisers or developers acquire new users or monetize digital traffic.

AppDiscovery is designed specifically for advertisers, and this tool finds potential new users by utilizing AppLovin's proprietary machine learning algorithms to place ads in the best spots. Conversely, the MAX product helps developers run bidding auctions for ad space in real time to maximize the revenue per impression they earn.

While AppDiscovery remains the primary revenue driver for the advertising segment, I believe that MAX has an opportunity to expand substantially over the coming years. The real-time bidding market is expected to grow at a 25% CAGR from now through 2029, with the sector expected to hit ~$52 billion by the end of that period.

The Adjust tool helps app developers track user behavior by giving them access to detailed reports and advanced analytics tools. This product can also be used to show developers where their users came from, as this shows which ads are working well and should continue to be run.

Finally, the Wurl product connects video content providers with connected TV platforms. This product comes from a company that AppLovin previously acquired, and there are obvious synergies here as AppLovin can handle ad delivery and targeting after Wurl connects these two providers.

The most powerful catalyst for AppLovin moving forward is the AXON platform, given the fact that the engine continuously improves as it is fed more data. The platform is constantly ingesting new streams of information from advertisers and app developers, and the tool uses reinforcement learning to deliver more personalized, better-targeted ads to consumers.

This creates a virtuous cycle as advertisers see better conversions due to ads being more targeted, which leads to them spending more on advertising, which continues to enhance the AXON platform as it is receiving more high-quality data.

Just recently, AppLovin completed its divestiture of its mobile gaming business, selling it to Tripledot Studios for a package including $150 million in cash, a $250 million promissory note with an 11% interest rate, and a 20% stake in Tripledot's parent company.

In my view, this is an excellent move as it aligns with AppLovin's clear strategic direction of becoming a pure-play software company. AppLovin's management team explained that they only ever owned and operated mobile games because it allowed them to develop the powerful machine learning algorithm that drives the advertising business today.

AppLovin is also exploring opportunities beyond the mobile gaming ad market. The company has recently seen traction in website-based advertising, particularly with e-commerce and fintech businesses, who have historically been incredibly reliant upon the incumbent players in the web advertising space (namely Google and Meta).

AppLovin is positioning its platform as a complementary channel for digital businesses to advertise through, which is why executives at the company believe marketing budgets will increase amongst advertisers as they begin to realize the power of AppLovin's machine learning and AI tools in helping them deliver solid ROAS figures.

Additionally, AppLovin is in the midst of rolling out a self-service dashboard, which I believe will contribute meaningfully to future growth. This dashboard promises to allow advertisers to directly run ad campaigns with enhanced control and automation features, which reduces the reliance advertisers will have on AppLovin's support teams.

This is incredibly important as there is a large number of advertisers who are waiting to be onboarded to the platform, and the introduction of a dashboard tool should increase the firm's ability to scale rapidly.

Management had previously estimated that web-based advertising would amount to roughly 10% of total advertising revenue in FY25; however, on the most recent earnings call, they indicated that the actual figure may exceed these targets if the self-service dashboard launch goes as planned.

Given that the company's web-based advertising business is already operating at over a $1 billion annual run rate, I believe it is very possible that the full year figure makes up more than a tenth of the advertising segment revenue.

Looking ahead, current estimates indicate that the total addressable market (TAM) for AppLovin could expand from ~$1.2 trillion to ~$3.8 trillion as the firm continues to diversify its business model and growth occurs in the web-based category.

Also, CEO Adam Foroughi recently noted that the web advertising market is over 10x the size of the mobile gaming market, which underscores the magnitude of this opportunity for AppLovin.

Financials

AppLovin reported strong Q1 FY25 results, with revenue coming in at $1.5 billion (up 40% YoY), and net income surged an astounding 144% to $576.4 million. The advertising segment was the standout performer, growing revenue 71% YoY to $1.16 billion and now accounting for approximately 78% of total revenue.

Comparatively, the Apps segment saw revenues decline around 14% YoY to $325 million. While this may seem like a cause for concern, this business is not central to the growth story of AppLovin, as evidenced by the recent divestiture of a large chunk of the mobile gaming segment.

AppDiscovery has remained the top-performing sub-segment within advertising, with revenue per install rising 49% YoY and total installs increasing by 22%. Together, these growth metrics contributed to the top-line expansion of the advertising segment.

Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was another strong point, rising 82% YoY to $1 billion. Given that the rise in EBITDA outpaced revenue growth, there was also a degree of margin expansion as the advertising segment's EBITDA margin hit an otherworldly 81%. Perhaps more impressive was the fact that net margins expanded by 16.5% to reach a whopping 38.8%, demonstrating the powerful operating leverage this business has.

In addition to impressive growth metrics, the company also exhibited strong cost discipline. Total operating costs only rose by around 14%, which was well below the top-line growth rate. Excluding the large $188.9 million goodwill impairment charge related to the Apps segment, operating costs actually declined by around 12% YoY.

Interest expenses fell YoY due to more favorable borrowing terms on the firm's debt balance, and other income more than tripled to $7.8 million (which both provided tailwinds to net income).

However, R&D spending was one line item that jumped out to me in a negative way. This expense declined 21% YoY, and now represents just 8% of total revenue. While declining costs are generally considered to be a positive, I view underinvestment in R&D spending as a concern, as the company is competing in one of the most cutthroat industries in the world.

The executive team needs to recognize the opportunity at hand, and I would prefer them to keep their foot on the gas when it comes to spending on developing new innovations.

Finally, management bought back nearly $1.2 billion worth of stock during the most recent quarter at an average share price of around $342 (which is below the current market share price at the time of writing).

The board has also authorized the repurchase of an additional $1.27 billion worth of shares, which could provide a powerful tailwind for EPS metrics in the coming quarters. Over the past year alone, fully diluted shares outstanding decreased by more than 1% despite equity awarded through SBC packages being exercised during that time frame.

Risks

While there's a lot to like here, I believe a number of risk factors remain present. First off, competitor Meta Platforms (META) has demonstrated strength through its competing Advantage+ platform, with 10% lower costs associated with generating qualified leads.

Meta is also experimenting with fully automated campaign creation features, using generative AI to generate extremely personalized ads. In my view, it may be challenging for AppLovin to carve out market share in the web-based advertising space if Meta continues to innovate and ends up creating a superior offering that delivers higher returns on ad spend.

Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) is another competitor in this market, as its Performance Max platform runs ads for clients across Google's entire digital ecosystem, allowing advertisers to feed in data to allow for enhanced targeting.

Additionally, Google's AdMob platform competes with AppLovin's MAX mediation tool. At the moment, customers view AdMob in a more favorable light when it comes to targeting (as well as the onboarding process); however, AppLovin is still preferred for their exceptional customer support.

Unity (U) operates in the sector as well through its Vector AI platform, which has shown strong signs in terms of helping advertisers attract new users and developers monetize their ads. Unity has historically lagged AppLovin in regards to innovation and adoption of AI; however, there is evidence that some developers have been migrating from MAX to Unity's LevelPlay.

I believe that this should be taken as a warning sign to the management team at AppLovin, as the firm's ~70% market share in the ad mediation market is under attack.

Finally, the Apps segment is in decline, and it will continue to shrink over the coming quarters as the company completes its divestiture of its mobile gaming IP. While this is an intentional move, it does create a sense of urgency for AppLovin to begin growing in the web-based advertising space.

This also increases the need for the self-service model to deliver on promises, which is the best avenue for the firm to make a big splash in the web-based advertising market.

Valuation

In order to determine AppLovin's intrinsic value, I created a discounted cash flow model. The perpetual growth rate I used in the model was 3% and my projection period covers the next ten years.

For my cost of capital, I first computed the cost of debt using the TTM interest expense, total debt, and tax rate information from Seeking Alpha. Next, I calculated the cost of equity using the yield on a 10-year U.S. treasury bond as a proxy for the risk-free rate, the stock's beta from S&P Capital IQ, and the equity risk premium from Professor Aswath Damodaran's website.

Below are my calculations for both WACC as well as the implied share price for the business:

Most of my estimates were in line with the consensus projections, although I was a bit more generous with both my revenue growth rates and operating margins in the later years of the projection.

I believe the company can remain extremely profitable as the AXON engine consistently improves and as large brands continue to gravitate towards their best-in-class advertising solution.

Based on these assumptions, I arrived at an equity value of ~$143.6 billion, which yields an implied share price of $413.87. Based on the current market share price, this represents around 17.2% upside, which is extremely solid given the strength of this business and the run-up we've already seen over the past year.

Conclusion

AppLovin is executing well, with strong Q1 FY25 results supporting investor optimism. The AXON platform also continues to deliver superior ad performance for customers, and new products such as the self-service dashboard position the company to continue growing over the medium to long term.

While the competitive landscape in the ad tech space remains intense, I believe that AppLovin's singular focus and specialization in the space should allow the company to capture more market share and upend the incumbent players to a degree, especially if web-based advertisers begin migrating to AppLovin's platform.

With a growing TAM and strong operating leverage, I believe the current market share price underestimates this business and its long-term potential. As such, I'm initiating a buy rating on AppLovin.

data.title: BANK:CA: The Cost Of Its Consistent 15.81% Monthly Payout
data.description:
One covered call ETF that has delivered impressive payouts in the last two years is the Evolve Canadian Banks and Lifecos Enhanced Yield Index Fund (TSX:BANK:CA). The fund currently yields 15.87% and has never cut its monthly payout since its inception in 2022. Moreover, these yields have been accompanied by positive total returns and outperformance over many of its competitors, making it an attractive investment for many income investors. These characteristics, however, can sound too good to be true. There are risks behind how BANK:CA generates its phenomenal returns and income that investors should be wary of, and they are risks that make other strategies potentially more appealing. Overall, however, I rate BANK:CA a Buy as its active management has demonstrated strong results that are attractive for Canadian income-focused investors.

BANK:CA's Phenomenal Performance

Since BANK:CA's inception on February 1, 2022, the fund has offered similar returns as the overall Canadian financials index represented by the iShares S&P/TSX Capped Financials Index ETF (XFN:CA). Although price performances have not recovered and sit at a loss of 19%, overall total returns have come close to XFN:CA's 38.80%.

In return for a slight underperformance, however, BANK:CA has offered a distribution yield frequently five times higher than that of XFN:CA and a monthly payout that has consistently grown over time. Dividend yields from BANK:CA have always exceeded 10% since its inception and currently sit above 15%. Monthly dividend payouts have increased from $0.07 per share to $0.11 for a TTM dividend growth rate of 10.73%.

There has not been a comparable ETF selling covered calls on Canadian financial companies that has generated the same level of returns as BANK:CA. The Hamilton Canadian Financials YIELD MAXIMIZER ETF (HMAX:CA) that I covered recently is similar, with its 13%+ yield and active management selling covered calls on various Canadian financial companies. The Global X Enhanced Equal Weight Canadian Banks Covered Call ETF (BKCL:CA) is also similar, with a strategy of selling covered calls on Canadian banks and a 14.77% yield. Neither of these funds, however, have been able to match BANK:CA in total returns and more importantly, have demonstrated an inability to sustain these large yields. Both have seen their distributions fall in the same time when BANK:CA has been increasing their payouts.

BANK:CA's Strategy

How is it possible that BANK:CA outperforms other similar ETFs with higher yields and increasing distributions? The answer boils down to three main factors: portfolio composition, a very active covered call approach, and leverage.

Comparing BANK:CA to Canadian financial indices and covered call ETFs is far from an apples-to-apples comparison due to its holdings. The fund tracks the Solactive Canadian Core Financials Equal Weight Index, which only consists of ten stocks. These ten stocks include Canada's Big Six Banks, Sun Life Financial (SLF:CA), Great-West Lifeco (GWO:CA), Power Corp of Canada (POW:CA), and Manulife Financial (MFC:CA). This is different from HMAX:CA, whose underlying holdings add on Brookfield Corp (BN:CA) and Intact Financial (IFC:CA) while removing GWO:CA and POW:CA. HMAX:CA is also different in that it weights its holdings by market cap, and both BKCL:CA and CIC:CA only track the Canadian Big Six Banks. Similarly, the broader XFN:CA consists of a far broader spectrum of Canadian financial companies in a market-cap weighting approach that have underperformed BANK:CA's underlying holdings on their own.

But while BANK:CA's underlying holdings have beaten XFN:CA and the underlying holdings of other Canadian financial covered call ETFs, it has not outperformed by much. A larger reason for BANK:CA's performance comes from its active covered call strategy. Per Evolve ETFs, there are four main considerations with its covered call strategy that differentiate the fund from competitors. First, the fund uses a tactical approach where the fund will frequently adjust how much of its portfolio is covered in its call options. BANK:CA will generally not have covered calls covering more than 33% of its portfolio and will do things like sell more covered calls during down markets to lower downside or fewer during bull markets to capture more upside.

Second, BANK:CA sells covered calls on individual stocks and an important differentiating factor separating the fund from something like HMAX:CA is that it operates with more freedom on its individual stock call options. The fund doesn't sell on all positions equally, but rather, more or less on certain holdings depending on factors like volatility. This allows BANK:CA to take advantage of situations that are independent to various holdings and maximize returns while paying large distributions. A good example is how The Toronto-Dominion Bank (TD:CA) faced a money laundering problem in the second half of 2024 that caused its price to drop and increased its volatility without affecting the other Canadian bank stocks. BANK:CA would have had an opportunity to capture increased premiums from TD:CA during their struggle while maintaining the upside in the other five banks that were unaffected.

Third, BANK:CA will also actively change their strike price between, but not limited to, 2-5% out-of-the-money ("OTM") on their options' contracts. This is very powerful when combined with a distribution target because the fund managers can look to layer multiple call contracts at different strike prices to guarantee the income while minimizing upside loss. For example, you can see how BANK:CA has done that with some of its BMO:CA call options where the fund managers layered two option contracts with different strike prices for slightly more upside if BMO:CA had traded sideways and landed in between the two strike prices.

Similarly, the fourth differentiating factor is BANK:CA's strategy to actively buy back options and adjust contracts at new strike prices to further minimize losing your upside. The idea is that if you have generated enough option premium for your target, even after you buy back your option, then there's no reason to keep holding the option and carry the risk of getting assigned.

These small efficiencies in BANK:CA's covered call strategy add up and are amplified when you add in leverage. BANK:CA calls itself an "enhanced" fund where it employs 25% cash leverage on its portfolio. This is a strategy that has become popular due to the nature of selling covered calls. Since selling covered calls is a strategy that can lower the overall volatility of your portfolio, income-focused investors often like to add leverage to boost overall returns and income while keeping risk at a tolerable level.

Tax-Efficient For The Canadian Investor

One little-known benefit of having leverage as well is that it provides BANK:CA with more opportunities to make use of tax-loss harvesting opportunities. For BANK:CA to maintain a 25% leveraged position, it can often end up in situations where it is selling assets at a loss when its underlying assets fall and the margin position grows bigger, while buying assets at higher prices as the assets grow in value and the margin position shrinks relative to the portfolio. While this is not inherently a good thing, it does allow BANK:CA to categorize much of its distribution as return of capital ("ROC"). This is a tax classification that essentially allows you to defer tax payments until your investment cost basis reaches zero or when you sell, at which time you will incur capital gains taxes. Capital gains taxes on your distributions are also generally efficient, as only 50% of your capital gains are counted and taxed at your ordinary income rates. This is also the type of distribution that BANK:CA has when it's not distributing ROC payments alongside the eligible dividends from its underlying holdings. As always, please consult a professional tax advisor for your specific situation.

Tax information for BANK:CA can be found here.

The Risks

Of course, BANK:CA is not a fund that comes without risk, and I wouldn't expect the fund to maintain 15%+ yields while capturing most of the upside in its underlying assets for a couple of reasons. The first is that leverage is a double-edged sword. It can amplify your losses just as it amplifies gains, but it also brings on the consideration of interest rates. Part of the reason BANK:CA has performed so well lately is that low interest rates lower BANK:CA's interest expenses. What happens if interest rates were higher? Fortunately, we do have that data. BANK:CA had been around for most of the 2022 interest rate hikes in Canada and all of 2023 when interest rates were at their highest in the last ten years.

The result was that BANK:CA underperformed its underlying assets on their own as well as the overall Canadian financials index. Volatility and drawdown levels were also consistently worse in BANK:CA compared to holding the underlying holdings by themselves and XFN:CA, which may dampen your view on BANK:CA if you invest in Canadian financials for their general lower volatility. The standard deviation for BANK:CA was 21.64% between March 2022 and December 2023 compared to 18.16% when you hold the underlying assets without leverage and 18.64% from XFN:CA. The maximum drawdown was also -20.22% compared to -16.38% and -16.20%.

An investment in BANK:CA also relies on the continual successful performance of its fund managers. If the fund managers set distribution targets that are too high, this could lead to taking on risks and selling call options that are far too aggressive. On the other hand, selling call options that are too conservative leads to lower dividend payouts. Investors should be prepared to accept either higher dividend payouts or total returns, but never expect both.

Income Investing Vs. Dividend Growth

Additionally, a third risk to consider is how selling covered calls can lead to situations where your shares are assigned. This is a major discussion point in my article on HMAX:CA, where I expressed my preference for holding Canadian financial companies directly without selling any covered calls. The reasoning behind that is because of the high and consistent dividend growth from Canadian financial companies. As certain funds have demonstrated, it is entirely possible that the dividends from Canadian financial companies eventually surpass the income generated from selling covered calls.

That preference has not changed, but the appeal of BANK:CA lies elsewhere. So far, the fund has demonstrated the ability and willingness to make consistent distributions. For Canadian income investors, this makes investing and managing finances very simple. Whenever BANK:CA share prices fall due to weakness in Canadian financial markets or other market factors, they're simply opportunities to grow your income stream faster and acquire shares at a lower price. Hence, I rate BANK:CA a Buy for Canadian income investors.

data.title: Floor & Decor: Building Tomorrow's Strength In Today's Downturn
data.description:
Since my last article on Floor & Decor Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:FND), its stock price has risen to around $80. However, the stock is still in a consolidation phase, which I don't see as yet as a clear upward trend. However, I see FND has grown stronger after Q1 2025, and I see some positive early signs of market recovery. Therefore, I would like to maintain my Buy rating.

FND is still dealing with near-term challenges

To recap, the Q1 2025 revenue grew 5.8% to 1.16B while operating income grew 8.3% which indicates an operating margin improvement of 10 basis points to 5.5%. Adjusted EBITDA increased 5.5% to $129.8 million, and EPS was $0.45, which is the high end of management's guidance range. Gross margin is surprisingly good, expanding 100 basis points to 43.8%. This is very impressive given an inflationary environment and Trump's tariff policy. In fact, if we exclude the extra cost of the new distribution center, the actual gross margin was 44.4%.

While these headline numbers look good, some concerning factors signal headwinds ahead. The comparable store sales (an important metric I watch) declined 1.8% indicating the business prospect is still weak. The management has updated their initial 2025 outlook with more conservative projections on almost all metrics (table below), and they have also reduced their new store opening target from 25 to 20 by 2025. All these changes are not a vote of confidence as they worry about a potential slowdown in the housing market and consumer spending moving forward.

The capability to execute makes FND stronger under pressure

Selling flooring products isn't easy. There are many moving parts in this business, such as supply chain, customer service, retail design, pricing, sizing, etc. FND needs to have both scale and speed in everything they are doing in order to win the competition. The recent pressure from inflation, tariffs, and high interest rates is a big test for FND management. Nevertheless, I am impressed by FND's execution during this period. In the past year, FND very quickly optimized its global supply chain, including diversifying vendors, improving routing and freight efficiency, and selling more high-margin products. Now, dependence on China source has reduced from 25% to 13%, and is expected to drop further to mid single digits. While leaving China isn't a unique strategy by FND, they are the fastest to execute. Competitors like Home Depot and Lowe's were moving at much lower speeds, as they are only talking about 'gradual diversification'.

While FND is at a much lower scale than The Home Depot, Inc. (HD) and Lowe's Companies, Inc. (LOW), it has always been able to maintain high gross margins. As the chart below indicates, FND's gross margin has been growing almost consistently (excluding the Covid period) and is significantly higher by 1k base points than HD and LOW. The recent macro headwinds even enlarged FND's lead.

Why I believe FND may continue to expand margins

Although FND is a relatively small company in a massive industry, it already has one of the largest scales in the flooring segment. Its stores have the largest square footage and richest selection. I think they already have a strong scale advantage inside the flooring category. As FND keeps getting bigger, the costs of adding additional business lines are getting lower. For example, the capex for new stores is guided around 200-235m for 2025, 25 stores. This indicates 8m per store (for a low-end estimate), which is lower than the 8.4m from last year.

Being simply bigger at scale, FND will receive more benefits through automation and standardization of its processes. The operational cost savings in comparable stores have achieved 40m last year alone for the company, which is achieved under an inflationary environment. The new distribution centers and ERP software systems proposed in 2025 should further elevate their efficiency and lead to more cost savings. Given the complexity of FND’s operations, the company still has many processes that could be optimized. I expect more cost savings in the future.

FND is actually keeping its brand equity during the downturn

As the housing transactions continue to decline, the demand for flooring should also go down. This could be seen from the monthly web traffic data (below). Although the FND website visitation number has a jump during March 2025, it is still in a slow declining trend thereafter.

However, if we look at unique visitors to the website, we can see a stabilization trend as the total unique consumers in June 2025 goes higher than in March. This clearly outperforms visitation numbers. I think FND attracted some new customers during the quarter, but the frequency of going to FND links was lower. Overall, FND as a brand isn’t weakened during the recent market downturn, as their customers are still with them.

Bottom Line

Currently, FND’s trailing P/E of 47x may look more expensive than Home Depot’s 23x and Lowe’s 17x. However, the company’s headline earnings are undermined by new store investments and other growth-related projects whose costs are not continuous. To calculate the true earnings power for last quarter, we should add back 38.5M in SGA spending related to new stores and 4.2M in pre-opening costs. This suggests an estimated earnings of 92M which could lower P/E multiples back to 20x. Given FND’s growth prospects (25 new stores per year) and competitive moat in the flooring industry, I don’t see it overvalued at all.

FND is a high-quality company that has durable and profitable business models. Their management is very experienced and capable in the flooring industry. They also seem to be shareholder-friendly as the company didn’t dilute shares as much and emphasized EPS and many other per share metrics. Once the housing market recovers, FND should be able to deliver outsized profits.

I am not so pessimistic about the macroenvironment. The new home sales in April 2025 are 10% more than last year, a growth rate we haven't seen for a while. As the Google search trend shown below, Flooring interests have been declining since 2021. But we actually started seeing a bounce in search recently. This indicates that people are actively looking for information related to flooring. I think a recovery of demand for flooring will soon occur in the US, which will certainly benefit FND. For the Q2 2025 earnings preview, I expect the company to deliver results at the high end of its guidance. I anticipate Q2 revenue reaching $1.20 billion with EPS approaching $0.55. Gross margin may be negatively affected by the new distribution centers, so I will give it a 43.8% estimate.

Regarding risks, I am worried about the staffing and labor issues in the US. I think skilled workers will be more and more difficult to find in the future. FND needs people with very specialized knowledge and experience in flooring. Working in FND stores was considered more ‘high-touch’ with customers and required longer training times before onboarding. As FND continues to open new stores in different geographies and expand their product offerings to cabinets/outdoors, etc., the complexity of running their stores increases. And they will need more people to handle already complex supply chains and serve customers. It is unknown if FND could still keep a lean operation when facing higher labor costs.

data.title: Staying The Course: Resisting Temptation In Volatile 2025 Markets
data.description:
By Teresa Keane and Walt Czaicki, CFA

Missing the Five Strongest Days in a Market Rally Can Ruin Returns

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.
*Annualized trailing three-year daily returns of the S&P 500 and MSCI EAFE and trailing three-year daily returns where the best five days are excluded from each rolling three-year period and are calculated every business day from January 2008 (trailing three years goes back to January 2005) through end of June 2025. The average of these returns is then calculated to give the results in the chart. MSCI EAFE returns are shown in US-dollar terms.As of June 30, 2025
Source: FactSet, MSCI, S&P and AllianceBernstein (AB)

Volatility often evokes emotional responses from investors. Two big sell-offs in early 2025 reminded us why it’s important to fight those responses and stay invested through downturns.

It’s perfectly human to feel fearful when the value of your investments is falling fast. However, trying to time the market can be counterproductive, as investors may forfeit strong gains that are common after volatility peaks.

Calculating the Cost of Market Timing

That timeless lesson was reinforced this year (Display). First, global stocks fell in the first quarter after DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) artificial intelligence breakthrough raised concern about the technology mega-caps. Then, in early April, equities tumbled in response to President Trump’s sweeping tariff plans, followed by a rapid recovery after a 90-day pause was announced a week later.

By quarter-end, equities touched record highs. However, when excluding the five best days of the recovery, returns were dramatically impaired. For the S&P 500, missing the five best days in the first half of 2025 translated to a 12.1% loss, compared with a 6.2% gain for the full period. For non-US stocks, the MSCI EAFE Index returned 2.0% in US dollar terms, excluding the five best days, versus a 19.4% gain for those who stayed invested. These trends echo long-term patterns observed over the last 20 years in equity markets, when missing the five best days over each rolling three-year period significantly weakened returns.

How to Resist the Fear Factor

Despite the rebound, headline risks remain. Trade wars, macroeconomic challenges and geopolitical risks could provoke more market volatility.

So how can investors combat the natural impulse to sell in turbulent markets? Defensive equities can help. In particular, we believe an active strategy that aims to curb losses in down markets and capture most (but not all) gains in up markets can deliver a smoother pattern of returns over time. Targeting select stocks that offer quality, stability and attractive prices (what we call “QSP”) can help a defensive allocation get a better balance of returns through market cycles. And for investors, understanding a portfolio’s philosophy and approach - and the pattern of returns that it aims to deliver - is especially important in a world of slower growth and market turmoil.

In our view, a defensive portfolio that eases the pain of losses in falling markets can help investors pass the biggest test when market conditions get rough. By resisting the temptation to sell at the wrong time, investors will dramatically improve the chances of achieving their long-term goals.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio management teams. Views are subject to revision over time.

data.title: 45 Barron's 2025 Mid-Year Pro-Picks: 3 Ideal July DiviDogs
data.description:
Foreword

This article is based on one Barron's Weekly article revealing 55 select stocks for the Mid-Year 2025. That article was "55 Picks From Our Roundtable Pros" by Lauren R. Rublin.

Roundtable Panelists & Picks

Barron's New-Year 2025 Roundtable panelists and their stock picks for this article:

Any collection of stocks is more clearly understood when subjected to this yield-based (dog catcher) analysis; these Barron's New-Year 2025 Pro Picks are perfect for the dogcatcher process.

Here is July 11 updated YCharts data for 45 of 55 stocks, of which 11 are "safer," with three of which live up to the dogcatcher 'ideal' in this collection. These complete the Barron's Mid-Year 2025 Pro-Picks collection.

The Ides of March 2020 plunge in the stock market took its toll on all stocks over five years ago. However, the sudden recovery in prices after the plunge by dividend stocks made the possibility of owning productive dividend shares from any collection more remote for first-time investors.

July 2025 shows three glimmers of light from the Barron's Roundtable stocks emerging as dogcatcher "safer" ideal candidates. They were AT&T (T), Tegna (TGNA), and Pitney Bowes (PBI), the "safer" survivors in the top ten by yield.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Estimated Top-Ten Barron's Round Table Mid-Year 2025 Picks Could Net 15.81% to 41.81% Gains By July 2026

Four of the top-ten Barron's Mid-Year 2025 Pro-Picks by-yield (tinted in the chart below) were also top gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year targets. Thus, the top yield dog strategy for this group, as graded by analyst estimates for this month, proved 40% accurate.

Estimated dividend returns from $1,000 invested in each of the highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points. However, one-year target prices by less than two analysts were not counted. The resulting ten probable best-profit-generating 2025 Barron's Mid-Year Pro Picks projected to July 11, 2026, by that reckoning, were:

Pitney Bowes netted $418.10 based on the median of target estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate is subject to risk/volatility 60% more than the market as a whole.

First American Financial (FAF) netted $386.17 based on the median of target estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate is subject to risk/volatility 24% more than the market as a whole.

Danaher (DHR) netted $252.10 based on the median of target estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility of 23% under the market as a whole.

Telephone and Data Systems (TDS) netted $251.89 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts plus dividends less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility of 48% under the market as a whole.

Becton Dickinson (BDX) netted $321.98 based on the median of target estimates from 14 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate is subject to risk/volatility of 69% under the market as a whole.

Tegna netted $198.79 based on the median of target prices estimated by 5 analysts, plus estimated dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate is subject to risk/volatility of 70% under the market as a whole.

Allstate (ALL) netted $178.58 based on the median of target estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate is subject to risk/volatility of 67% below the market as a whole.

AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) netted $175.82 based on estimates from 12 analysts, plus dividends less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate is subject to risk/volatility of 28% greater than the market as a whole.

Annaly Capital Management (NLY) netted $175.75 based on estimates from 11 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate is subject to risk/volatility of 22% over the market as a whole.

Sinclair (SBGI) netted $158.06 based on dividends plus a median target price estimate from 7 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate is subject to risk/volatility 30% greater than the market as a whole.

The average net-gain in dividend and price was 24.29% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these ten 2025 Barron's RoundTable Pro-Picks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 12% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

The "dog" moniker was earned by stocks exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) and their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More specifically, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs."

Top 50 Barron's RoundTable 2025 Dividend Pro Picks By Broker Targets

This scale of broker-estimated upside (or downside) for stock prices provides a measure of market popularity. Note: no broker coverage or 1 broker coverage produced a zero score on the above scale. This scale can be taken as an emotional component as opposed to the strictly monetary and objective dividend/price yield-driven report below. As noted above, these scores may also be taken as contrarian.

Top 50 Barron's RoundTable 2025 Pro Picks By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): Ten Top Stocks By Yield Are Dogs of Barron's Mid-Year 2025 RoundTable ProPicks

Top ten equities selected 7/11/25 by yield represented six of eleven Morningstar sectors and two ETFs.

First place was secured by the first of three financial sector members, AGNC Investment [1]. The other two placed second, and eighth, Annaly Capital [2], and First American [8].

Third and sixth places were secured by two communication services representatives, Sinclair [3] and AT&T [6].

The two exchange traded funds penetrated the top ten Barrons 2025 Mid-Yr Pro-RoundTable-Picked list in fourth, and seventh places, Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) [4], and Franklin Dynamic Municipal Bond ETF (FLMI) [7].

Thereafter, one consumer staples member placed fifth, Target (TGT) [5]. Then the lone utilities representative placed ninth, American Electric (AEP) [9].

Finally a consumer discretionary representative placed tenth, Golden Entertainment (GDEN) [10], which completed the top ten Barron's Mid-Year RT 2025 Pro-Picksfor July.

Barron's Mid-Year 2025 RoundTable ProPick Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 7/11/25-6

The above chart reveals 26 Barron's New-Year 2025 RoundTable ProPickdividenddogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the list is AGNC at $6.47 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive is META at $343.29 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21):

Eight of Twenty-six July Dividend-Paying Barron's Mid-Year 2025 RoundTable ProPicks Show Negative Free-Cash-Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Eight dividend-paying Barron's Pro-Picked stocks so infected are AGNC, Annaly Capital, Sinclair, Enterpise Product Partners, Janus Henderson AAA CEIC ETF, Franklin Dynamic Municipal Bond ETF, American Electric, NextEra Energy, Amaren, NiSource, and CenterPoint Energy. These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, executive uncertainty, wars of any sort, and tariffs, too.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases, or Re-Rates Could Get All Ten Top-Yield Barron's Mid-Year 2025 RoundTable ProPick Stocks Back to "Fair-Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, six in the top ten are already ideally priced. Beside those six, already in the ideal zone, four more top-yield stocks are within 42% of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise their dividend or split shares. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts; just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to the sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (21-30) Ten Barron's RoundTable Mid-Year 2025 Pro Pix Showed 10.37% to 40.5% Upsides To July 11, 2026, With (31) Two -1.19% to -1.59% Losers

To quantify top-yield rankings, analyst median-price target estimates provided a "market sentiment" gauge of upside potential. Added to the simple high-yield metrics, analyst median price target estimates became another tool to dig out bargains.

Analysts Estimated A 5.02% Advantage For 5 Highest Yield, Lowest Priced of Top-Ten Barron's 2025 Pro-Pix To July, 2026

Ten top Barron's Round Table Mid-Year 2025 Pro-Picks were culled by yield for this monthly update. Yield (dividend/price) results verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted above, top-ten Barron's Mid-Year RT 2025 Pro-Picked Dogs tagged 7/11/25, showing the highest dividend yields, represented six of eleven sectors in the Morningstar scheme, plus two ETFs.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Estimated 5 Lowest-Priced Of Ten Highest-Yield Barron's NY RT 2025 Pro Picks (33) Delivering 13.34% Vs. (34) 12.71% Net Gains by All Ten by July, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Barron's Mid-Year RT 2025 Pro-Pix by yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 5.02% more gain than $5,000 invested as $.5k in all ten. The seventh lowest-priced Barron's Mid-Year RT 2025 Pro-Pick, First American, was projected to deliver the best net gain of 38.62%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield Barron's Mid-Year RT 2025 Pro-Picks were: AGNC Investment; Sinlair; Annaly Capital; Franklin Dynamic Muni Bond ETF; and AT&T, with prices ranging from $9.08 to $29.26.

The five higher-priced top-yield Barron's NY RT 2025 Pro-Pix for MaY 15 were: Golden Entertainment; Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF; First American; Target; and American Electric, whose prices ranged from $32.59 to $105.02.

This distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment," gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market. Caution is advised since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

Afterword

If somehow you missed the suggestion of the stocks ripe for picking at the start of the article, here is a repeat at the end:

In the current market advance, dividends from $1K invested in the 'Safer' stocks listed above exceeded their single share prices, as of 7/11/25.

As we are past the fifth anniversary of the 2020 Ides of March dip, the time to snap up these top yield Barron's NY Mid-Year 2025 Pro Picks is now... unless another big bearish drop in price looms ahead. (At which time your strategy would be to add to your holdings.)

To learn which of these eight ideally priced opportunities are "safer" to buy (that is, having ready cash to pay dividends), read on.

Recent vs Fair Top Ten Barron's NY RT 2025 Pro-Pick Prices

Since one of the top-ten Barron's NY RT 2025 Pro-Picks is priced less than the annual dividends paid out from a $1K investment, the following top chart shows the dollar and percentage shift required for eight at recent prices to achieve fair pricing for all ten. The recent prices are documented in the middle chart, and the fair prices are revealed in the bottom chart.

The top chart is an indicator of how low the seven non-ideal "safer" dividend stocks must adjust price in order to conform to the standard of dividends from $1K invested exceeding the current single share price.

All 45 Barron's Mid-Year RT 2025 ProPix Stocks Alphabetically

The analysis above focuses primarily on the top yield "safer" 16 Barron's Mid-Year 2025 Pro-Picked selections. Below is the list of all 65 stocks, by alpha/numeric listing.

Below is July 11 updated YCharts data for 26 dividend-paying stocks of 55 named and the 16 'safer' choices, determined by free cash flow yields in excess of dividend yields.

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): Sixteen Top Stocks By Yield Are The 2025-26 Barron's Pro NY RT 'Safer' Dividend Dog Pack

Any collection of stocks is more clearly understood when subjected to this yield-based (dog catcher) analysis. This Barrons NY RT Pro list of dividend stocks is perfect for the dogcatcher process.

The Dogcatcher Ideal Barron's "Safer" Dogs For July

Three stocks emerged as a Barron's 'safer' dividend Mid-Year 2025 stock dogcatcher ideal candidates. They were: AT&T; Tegna; Pitney Bowes. Their prices settled at $13.74, $11.61, and $11, respectively, below the annual dividend payout from $1K invested.

One more is within $9.87 of joining the dogcatcher ideal "safer" team, First American.

Another two are within $29 of making the tipping point. They are, Telephone and Data just $21.10 high of the mark, and Fox only $28.08 out of range.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your safest "Safer" Dividend Barron's Mid-Year dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.
